title: Trauma and Shame: The Body's Most Toxic Emotion
excerpt: Explore how trauma and shame hijack the body, why shame is uniquely toxic, and how somatic healing can free you from their grip.
body:
Understanding Trauma and Shame: The Silent Saboteurs

If you’ve done therapy and still feel stuck, it’s time to look deeper—not just at your thoughts or memories, but at how trauma and shame live in your body. Shame is often called the body’s most toxic emotion for good reason. Unlike fear or anger, shame attacks your very sense of self, embedding itself in your nervous system and shaping how you experience the world.

What Is Shame, Really?

Shame is not just feeling bad about something you did. It’s a deep, pervasive sense that you are fundamentally flawed or unworthy. According to Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, shame is a core element of trauma that alters how the brain and body respond to stress and connection.

Unlike guilt, which is about behavior, shame is about identity. It tells you, "I am bad," not "I did something bad." This distinction is critical because shame shuts down our ability to regulate emotions and engage socially—two capacities essential for healing.

How Trauma Breeds Shame in the Body

When trauma occurs—whether from abuse, neglect, or overwhelming life events—the nervous system shifts into survival mode. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma disrupts the autonomic nervous system, often causing people to freeze or dissociate, which are common responses to overwhelming threat.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic trauma therapy, highlights that trauma doesn’t just live in the mind but is stored in the body’s muscular and nervous systems. Shame becomes embedded in bodily sensations—tightness in the chest, a sinking feeling in the gut, or a constricted throat. These sensations become triggers, reinforcing the toxic shame cycle.

Shame’s Physical Imprint

  • Muscle tension and chronic pain
  • Disrupted breathing and shallow chest respiration
  • Immune system suppression
  • Gut dysregulation and digestive issues
  • Freeze responses and dissociation

Why Shame Is More Toxic Than Other Emotions

Gabor Maté, known for his work on trauma and addiction, argues that shame is at the root of many chronic health conditions and addictive behaviors because it attacks the self at its core. Shame isolates people, cutting off the social engagement system that Deb Dana, an expert in Polyvagal Theory, identifies as crucial for feeling safe and connected.

This isolation increases the risk of depression, anxiety, and self-harm. Shame also impairs neural integration, as described by Dan Siegel, limiting our capacity to regulate emotions and respond flexibly to stress.

“Shame is the most toxic emotion because it attacks the essence of who we are, shutting down our nervous system’s ability to connect and heal.” — Bessel van der Kolk

The Neuroscience Behind Shame and Trauma

Francine Shapiro, the founder of EMDR therapy, explains how traumatic memories and their associated emotions like shame are stored in fragmented and non-verbal parts of the brain. When triggered, these fragments activate intense shame responses without conscious awareness.

The amygdala, responsible for threat detection, becomes hyperactive, while the prefrontal cortex, which governs rational thought and self-reflection, is underactive. This imbalance locks the individual in a loop of shame and fear, making it difficult to break free without targeted intervention.

Somatic Approaches to Healing Shame

Traditional talk therapy alone often misses the body-based nature of shame. Somatic therapies, informed by the work of Peter Levine and Pat Ogden, emphasize tracking bodily sensations, breathwork, and movement to discharge trapped trauma energy.

Here are somatic strategies that can help regulate the nervous system and dissolve shame:

  • Body Awareness: Noticing where shame lives in your body without judgment.
  • Resourcing: Activating sensations of safety and connection to counterbalance shame.
  • Grounding Techniques: Engaging feet on the floor, feeling physical support to stabilize the nervous system.
  • Breath Regulation: Using slow, deep breathing to stimulate the vagus nerve and calm the system.
  • Movement and Expression: Gentle movements or expressive arts to release tension and reclaim agency.

Internal Family Systems and Shame

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model sheds light on how shame often manifests as an internal "exile" part—a vulnerable, wounded self hidden away by protective parts that try to keep us safe but can increase shame through criticism or avoidance.

IFS offers a compassionate framework to unblend from these protective parts and bring healing attention to the exiled shame parts, fostering self-compassion and integration.

Practical Steps to Start Healing Shame Today

  • Practice Mindful Body Scans: Spend 5-10 minutes daily simply noticing sensations without trying to change them.
  • Journal Your Shame Triggers: Write down moments when shame arises and what bodily sensations accompany it.
  • Engage in Safe Relational Connection: Reach out to trusted friends or support groups to counter isolation.
  • Try Somatic Exercises: Simple breathwork or grounding exercises can shift nervous system states.
  • Seek Somatic Therapy: Work with trauma-informed therapists trained in somatic approaches for guided healing.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Your Body from Shame

Shame is not just a feeling to be managed; it’s a toxic emotion that rewires your nervous system and distorts your sense of self. Trauma and shame are deeply intertwined, locking the body in cycles of fear, isolation, and shutdown.

By understanding shame’s somatic roots and using body-centered healing methods, you can begin to unravel this toxic emotion. As Bessel van der Kolk and his colleagues have shown, the body holds the key to unlocking trauma’s grip—and with compassionate, informed care, you can reclaim your sense of worth, connection, and vitality.

Remember: healing shame is not about erasing your past but about learning to inhabit your body with safety and kindness in the present.

References

  • van der Kolk, Bessel. The Body Keeps the Score. Viking, 2014.
  • Porges, Stephen. The Polyvagal Theory. W.W. Norton, 2011.
  • Ogden, Pat. Trauma and the Body. W.W. Norton, 2006.
  • Maté, Gabor. In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. Knopf, 2008.
  • Dana, Deb. The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. W.W. Norton, 2018.
  • Siegel, Dan. The Developing Mind. Guilford Press, 2012.
  • Shapiro, Francine. EMDR Therapy and Adjunctive Treatments. Guilford Press, 2017.
  • Schwartz, Richard. Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press, 1995.

faqs.q: How does shame differ from guilt in trauma?
faqs.a: Shame attacks your identity, making you feel fundamentally flawed, while guilt focuses on specific behaviors. Shame is more toxic because it impacts your self-worth and nervous system regulation.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help with shame?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapy works directly with the body’s stored sensations and nervous system states, helping to release trapped shame and trauma beyond just talking.

faqs.q: Why does shame cause physical symptoms?
faqs.a: Shame activates the nervous system’s threat response, leading to muscle tension, disrupted breathing, and other bodily sensations as the body tries to protect itself.

faqs.q: Is shame always linked to trauma?
faqs.a: While not all shame comes from trauma, traumatic experiences often embed shame deeply in the body and psyche, making it harder to resolve without somatic approaches.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma and shame are stored in the body and offers insight into healing from a somatic perspective.

metaTitle: Trauma and Shame: Understanding the Body’s Toxic Emotion
metaDescription: Learn why shame is the body’s most toxic emotion linked to trauma and how somatic healing can help free you from its grip.
author: The Oracle Lover
